content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Yogi Atmo on UnsplashEvery layoff memo in tech now reads as if it came from the same template. Rapid advancements in artificial intelligence. Smaller teams doing more. A new way of working. The language arrives polished, forward-looking, and strangely uniform across companies with wildly different businesses, and that uniformity is the first tell. When Cloudflare, Cisco, Snap, Coinbase, and Block all reach for the same explanation in the same quarter, you are watching a narrative propagate, not a technology.
This is not an AI transformation story. This is a margin-management story wearing an AI lanyard.
The budget line gives it away
Look at where the money goes after the cuts. The four largest tech companies have guided for a combined capital expenditure of roughly $700 billion this year, nearly double last year, almost all of it directed toward data centers, chips, and AI infrastructure. Meanwhile, the layoff trackers have logged well over 100,000 tech job cuts in the first half of the year, running at nearly twice last year’s daily pace.
Those two numbers describe one transaction. Payroll is the highest controllable cost on the books, and infrastructure buildouts of this scale need funding that does not wreck quarterly margins. Cutting headcount and buying GPUs with the savings is capital reallocation. The AI framing dresses it up as a strategy. Meta posted revenue growth north of thirty percent, then cut thousands of roles while raising its infrastructure spending guidance in the same breath. Oracle executed the largest single cut of the year shortly after reporting strong earnings and announcing a multi-billion-dollar data center expansion. Companies in financial distress cut jobs. Companies funding a buildout also cut jobs, and the second group has learned that saying “AI” sounds better than saying “we found the money in payroll.”
The story changed but the pressure never did
Block offers the cleanest specimen. In early 2025, leadership told employees directly that the cuts underway had nothing to do with replacing people with AI. A year later, the shareholder letter attributed the elimination of roughly forty percent of the workforce to AI tools making those roles unnecessary. The business pressure across those two announcements stayed constant. The only thing that changed was which explanation played better.
The outplacement data backs up the skepticism. Challenger, Gray & Christmas, which has tracked layoff reasons for decades, ranks AI as only the fifth-most-cited driver of this year’s cuts, behind market conditions, restructuring, closures, and plain cost-cutting. Their own executives describe the mechanism precisely: budgets are shifting from payroll to AI investment, regardless of whether a machine has replaced any specific worker. Even the people building the technology have stopped playing along. Sam Altman has publicly said that companies are blaming AI for layoffs they would have made anyway, a pattern he calls "AI washing." Deutsche Bank analysts predicted in January that this would define the year. They were right.
The most damning detail sits in the operational reality. Wharton’s Peter Cappelli has pointed out that most companies announcing AI-driven cuts have not deployed anything that covers the eliminated work. They cut first and hope the tooling catches up. Forrester reached the same conclusion in institutional language: the mature, vetted AI applications supposedly replacing these roles mostly do not exist yet inside the organizations making the claims.
Why transformation leaders should care about someone else’s press release
The framing matters because it travels. Boards read these announcements and ask their own executives why headcount has not yet come down. CIOs inherit mandates built on fiction, then are held accountable for productivity gains that the fiction promised but the tooling cannot yet deliver. Anyone who has watched shifting executive attention orphan a transformation initiative knows how this ends: the cuts are permanent, the AI capability arrives late or never, and the remaining staff absorbs the work while the press release ages badly.
The practical discipline is simple. Read the filing, not the memo. When AI appears as a material claim in an SEC document, backed by deployed systems and measured output, take it seriously. When it shows up in a farewell email alongside record capex guidance, you are looking at a financing mechanism with better branding. The gap between those two documents is where the truth lives, and the executives writing the memos are counting on nobody checking.
AI will reshape work. Some of these cuts are real displacement, and pretending otherwise helps no one. But the burden of proof belongs on the press release, and right now most of them cannot carry it.

Will Kelly is a technical content strategist focused on AI enablement. He’s spent 15+ years in enterprise technology content, with 300+ bylines across major trade publications. Will writes about AI adoption, content operations, and the gap between vendor promises and enterprise reality. A dyslexic thinker, he credits pattern recognition as his edge. He writes Will’s Newsletter on Substack.
